problems.title: Exercise 7.9: the html template package to
problems.statement: Practice using the html/template package (§4.6) to replace printTracks with a function that displays the tracks as an HTML table. Practice using the solution to the previous exercise to arrange that each click on a column head makes an HTTP request to sort the table.
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 7.9, **the html template package to**, is a practice rep for http, sort, html in Interfaces. The surface task is: Practice using the html/template package (§4.6) to replace printTracks with a function that displays the tracks as an HTML table. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is a project exercise, there may not be one hidden-test answer. The win is a small reproducible program plus proof that the observed behavior matches the goal.

### Go mental model

- HTTP code has two halves: constructing the request and interpreting the response. Keep errors, status, headers, and body handling distinct.

- Sorting requires a comparison rule. Make that rule match the domain, then let the sort package handle ordering mechanics.

- HTML tasks are tree or token-stream tasks. Separate traversal from the decision about which nodes matter.

### Tiny check

- Input: Book Exercise 7.9
  Output: A working Go program or function demonstrating the requested behavior.

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. problems.title: Exercise 7.11: additional handlers so that clients
problems.statement: Extend additional handlers so that clients can create, read, update, and delete database entries. For example, a request of the form /update?item=socks&price=6 will update the price of an item in the inventory and report an error if the item does not exist or if the price is invalid. (Warning: this change introduces concurrent variable updates.)

problems.title: Exercise 7.14: Define new concrete type that
problems.statement: Define a new concrete type that satisfies the Expr interface and provides a new operation such as computing the minimum value of its operands. Since the Parse function does not create instances of this new type, to use it you will need to construct a syntax tree directly (or extend the parser).

problems.title: Exercise 7.17: xmlselect so that elements may
problems.statement: Extend xmlselect so that elements may be selected not just by name, but by their attributes too, in the manner of CSS, so that, for instance, an element like <div id="page" class="wide"> could be selected by a matching id or class as well as its name.

problems.title: Exercise 7.18: Using the token based decoder
problems.statement: Using the token-based decoder API, write a program that will read an arbitrary XML document and construct a tree of generic nodes that represents it. Nodes are of two kinds: CharData nodes represent text strings, and Element nodes represent named elements and their attributes. Each element node has a slice of child nodes. You may find the following declarations helpful. import "encoding/xml"
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 7.18, **Using the token based decoder**, is a practice rep for strings, slice, interface, xml in Interfaces. The surface task is: Using the token-based decoder API, write a program that will read an arbitrary XML document and construct a tree of generic nodes that represents it. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is a project exercise, there may not be one hidden-test answer. The win is a small reproducible program plus proof that the observed behavior matches the goal.

### Go mental model

- Strings are immutable byte sequences. Decide whether the task is about bytes, runes, words, prefixes, or joined pieces before choosing helpers.

- Slices are views over arrays. Track length, capacity, and whether two slices might share the same backing array.

- Interfaces describe required behavior. Name the method set you need, then let concrete types satisfy it without ceremony.

- XML tasks are stream or tree tasks. Keep element names, attributes, nesting, and text handling as separate decisions.

### Tiny check

- Input: Book Exercise 7.18
  Output: A working Go program or function demonstrating the requested behavior.

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. Once the hand prediction is clear, the implementation usually becomes a direct translation: choose the right data shape, loop or call the helper that matches the shape, and return the result without extra cleverness.

### What mastery feels like

You understand this exercise when you can explain why each variable exists, what invariant is maintained while the code runs, and which edge case would break a sloppy solution. Do not just aim for green tests; aim for the moment where the code reads like the problem statement in Go form.
problems.approach:
### Solve path for exercise 7.18
1. Rephrase the task in one sentence using your own words.
2. Start with the smallest runnable version, even if it only handles one tiny case.
3. Run it and write down the command or input you used.
4. Expand the implementation until it demonstrates the full behavior.
5. Keep the proof: command, sample input or file, observed output, and one note explaining why it satisfies the exercise.
### Self-check before calling it done
- Can you point to the line where the main idea happens?
- Can you explain what happens for an empty, tiny, or boundary-shaped input?
- Did you keep I/O or demonstration code separate from the value transformation where possible?
- If you came back tomorrow, would the variable names remind you of the problem rather than the mechanics?
pitfalls: Changing too much of the original example at once.
Ignoring errors because the happy path worked once.
Treating open-ended book exercises like single-output puzzles instead of reproducible programs.
hints: Find the chapter example this exercise modifies.
Make the smallest visible change first.
Keep a command or sample input that proves your solution works.

problems.howItWorks:
### How it works

The target behavior is: Using the token-based decoder API, write a program that will read an arbitrary XML document and construct a tree of generic nodes that represents it. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

A good solution usually has this flow:

1. Normalize the input into a shape that is easy to reason about.

2. Apply one clear rule repeatedly or directly, depending on the task.

3. Keep intermediate names honest so the code explains what each value means.

4. Build the smallest runnable demonstration that shows the behavior. For project exercises, the proof matters as much as the code.

5. Record the command, input, and observed output so you can tell whether later edits changed the behavior.

### Why this approach works

- Strings are immutable byte sequences. Decide whether the task is about bytes, runes, words, prefixes, or joined pieces before choosing helpers.

- Slices are views over arrays. Track length, capacity, and whether two slices might share the same backing array.

- Interfaces describe required behavior. Name the method set you need, then let concrete types satisfy it without ceremony.

- XML tasks are stream or tree tasks. Keep element names, attributes, nesting, and text handling as separate decisions.

### Debugging lens

If the answer is wrong, do not randomly rearrange code. Print or inspect the value after each stage of the flow: input shape, transformed shape, final result. Most mistakes in these exercises come from using the right tool at the wrong boundary, or from mixing representation details with the core rule.
problems.syntax:
### Syntax you need
Core shape:
```go
func Solve() string
```
- For a project entry, you may keep this shape as a helper, but you can also add a small `main` when you need a runnable proof.
### Imports
- No import is required by the starter code unless your implementation chooses a helper package.
### Patterns to reach for
- Strings are immutable byte sequences. Decide whether the task is about bytes, runes, words, prefixes, or joined pieces before choosing helpers.
- Slices are views over arrays. Track length, capacity, and whether two slices might share the same backing array.
- Interfaces describe required behavior. Name the method set you need, then let concrete types satisfy it without ceremony.
- XML tasks are stream or tree tasks. Keep element names, attributes, nesting, and text handling as separate decisions.
### Read the signature out loud
The parameters are the only facts your function gets for free. The return type is the promise your function must keep. When you get stuck, read the signature as a sentence and ask what value would satisfy that promise for the smallest sample input.

problems.title: Exercise 8.1: Clock Time Parse
problems.statement: Given HH:MM:SS strings, return them sorted as seconds since midnight.
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 8.1, **Clock Time Parse**, is a practice rep for channels, time in Goroutines and Channels. The surface task is: Given HH:MM:SS strings, return them sorted as seconds since midnight.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is judgeable, the app can check a focused `Solve` function. That is a gift: you can ignore command-line plumbing for a moment and concentrate on the exact value transformation.

### Go mental model

- Channels communicate values and also communicate timing. Ask which goroutine owns sending, which owns receiving, and how the receiver learns that work is done.

- Time code is easier when durations, deadlines, and timestamps are kept as separate ideas.

### Tiny check

- Input: 00:00:10, 00:00:02
  Output: [2,10]

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. Once the hand prediction is clear, the implementation usually becomes a direct translation: choose the right data shape, loop or call the helper that matches the shape, and return the result without extra cleverness.

### What mastery feels like

You understand this exercise when you can explain why each variable exists, what invariant is maintained while the code runs, and which edge case would break a sloppy solution. Do not just aim for green tests; aim for the moment where the code reads like the problem statement in Go form.
problems.approach:
### Solve path for exercise 8.1
1. Rephrase the task in one sentence using your own words.
2. Keep the core function compatible with `func Solve(times []string) []int`.
3. Work from the smallest sample input first. Predict the output by hand.
4. Implement the direct transformation. Prefer clear names and boring control flow.
5. Run once for feedback, then Submit when the examples and edge cases make sense.
### Self-check before calling it done
- Can you point to the line where the main idea happens?
- Can you explain what happens for an empty, tiny, or boundary-shaped input?
- Did you keep I/O or demonstration code separate from the value transformation where possible?
- If you came back tomorrow, would the variable names remind you of the problem rather than the mechanics?

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. Once the hand prediction is clear, the implementation usually becomes a direct translation: choose the right data shape, loop or call the helper that matches the shape, and return the result without extra cleverness.

problems.solutionCode:
import ("sort"; "strconv"; "strings")
func Solve(times []string) []int { out:=[]int{}; for _,t:=range times { p:=strings.Split(t,":"); if len(p)!=3 {continue}; h,_:=strconv.Atoi(p[0]); m,_:=strconv.Atoi(p[1]); s,_:=strconv.Atoi(p[2]); out=append(out,h*3600+m*60+s) }; sort.Ints(out); return out }

3. Keep intermediate names honest so the code explains what each value means.

4. Return exactly the type promised by the `Solve` signature. Avoid printing from `Solve` unless the signature or task explicitly asks for output text.

5. Let the local tests cover normal and edge cases after your hand-check makes sense.

### Why this approach works

problems.title: Exercise 8.2: concurrent File Transfer Protocol FTP
problems.statement: Implement a concurrent File Transfer Protocol (FTP) server. The server should interpret commands from each client such as cd to change directory, ls to list a directory, get to send the contents of a file, and close to close the connection. You can use the standard ftp command as the client, or write your own.
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 8.2, **concurrent File Transfer Protocol FTP**, is a practice rep for file, server in Goroutines and Channels. The surface task is: Implement a concurrent File Transfer Protocol (FTP) server. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is a project exercise, there may not be one hidden-test answer. The win is a small reproducible program plus proof that the observed behavior matches the goal.

### Go mental model

- File work has a boundary: open/read/scan at the edge, then pass ordinary strings, bytes, or lines into the core logic.

- Server code should keep handler state explicit. Parse the request, call focused logic, then write one clear response.

### Tiny check

problems.title: Exercise 8.3: In netcat3 the interface value
problems.statement: In netcat3, the interface value conn has the concrete type *net.TCPConn, which represents a TCP connection. A TCP connection consists of two halves that may be closed independently using its CloseRead and CloseBuild methods. Adapt the main goroutine of netcat3 to close only the write half of the connection so that the program will continue to print the final echoes from the reverb1 server even after the standard input has been closed. (Doing this for the reverb2 server is harder; see Exercise 8.4.)
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 8.3, **In netcat3 the interface value**, is a practice rep for echo, server, interface, goroutine in Goroutines and Channels. The surface task is: In netcat3, the interface value conn has the concrete type *net.TCPConn, which represents a TCP connection. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is a project exercise, there may not be one hidden-test answer. The win is a small reproducible program plus proof that the observed behavior matches the goal.

### Go mental model

- Echo-style problems are about preserving or reshaping order. Build the output from left to right so the code mirrors what the user sees.

- Server code should keep handler state explicit. Parse the request, call focused logic, then write one clear response.

- Interfaces describe required behavior. Name the method set you need, then let concrete types satisfy it without ceremony.

- A goroutine is independent work. Make its lifetime and result path visible so it cannot leak or silently fail.

### Tiny check

- Input: Book Exercise 8.3
  Output: A working Go program or function demonstrating the requested behavior.

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. Once the hand prediction is clear, the implementation usually becomes a direct translation: choose the right data shape, loop or call the helper that matches the shape, and return the result without extra cleverness.

### What mastery feels like

You understand this exercise when you can explain why each variable exists, what invariant is maintained while the code runs, and which edge case would break a sloppy solution. Do not just aim for green tests; aim for the moment where the code reads like the problem statement in Go form.

problems.title: Exercise 8.4: Pipeline Squares

problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

problems.title: Exercise 8.8: Using select statement add timeout
problems.statement: Using a select statement, add a timeout to the echo server from Section 8.3 so that it disconnects any client that shouts nothing within 10 seconds.
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 8.8, **Using select statement add timeout**, is a practice rep for echo, server in Goroutines and Channels. The surface task is: Using a select statement, add a timeout to the echo server from Section 8.3 so that it disconnects any client that shouts nothing within 10 seconds. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? ### Go mental model

- Echo-style problems are about preserving or reshaping order. Build the output from left to right so the code mirrors what the user sees.

- Server code should keep handler state explicit. Parse the request, call focused logic, then write one clear response.

problems.title: Exercise 8.9: version of du that computes

problems.title: Exercise 8.10: HTTP requests may be cancelled
problems.statement: HTTP requests may be cancelled by closing the optional Cancel channel in the http.Request struct. Adapt the web crawler of Section 8.6 to support cancellation. Hint: the http.Get convenience function does not give you an opportunity to customize a Request. Instead, create the request using http.NewRequest, set its Cancel field, then per- form the request by calling http.DefaultClient.Do(req).
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 8.10, **HTTP requests may be cancelled**, is a practice rep for http, channel in Goroutines and Channels. The target behavior is: HTTP requests may be cancelled by closing the optional Cancel channel in the http.Request struct. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

A good solution usually has this flow:

1. Normalize the input into a shape that is easy to reason about.

2. Apply one clear rule repeatedly or directly, depending on the task.

3. Keep intermediate names honest so the code explains what each value means.

4. Build the smallest runnable demonstration that shows the behavior. For project exercises, the proof matters as much as the code.

5. Record the command, input, and observed output so you can tell whether later edits changed the behavior.

### Why this approach works

- HTTP code has two halves: constructing the request and interpreting the response. Keep errors, status, headers, and body handling distinct.

- Channels communicate values and also communicate timing. Ask which goroutine owns sending, which owns receiving, and how the receiver learns that work is done.

### Debugging lens

If the answer is wrong, do not randomly rearrange code. Print or inspect the value after each stage of the flow: input shape, transformed shape, final result. Most mistakes in these exercises come from using the right tool at the wrong boundary, or from mixing representation details with the core rule.

### Imports
- No import is required by the starter code unless your implementation chooses a helper package.
### Patterns to reach for
- HTTP code has two halves: constructing the request and interpreting the response. Keep errors, status, headers, and body handling distinct.
- Channels communicate values and also communicate timing. Ask which goroutine owns sending, which owns receiving, and how the receiver learns that work is done.
### Read the signature out loud
The parameters are the only facts your function gets for free. The return type is the promise your function must keep. When you get stuck, read the signature as a sentence and ask what value would satisfy that promise for the smallest sample input.

problems.title: Exercise 8.11: Following the approach of mirroredQuery
problems.statement: Following the approach of mirroredQuery in Section 8.4.4, implement a vari- ant of fetch that requests several URLs concurrently. As soon as the first response arrives, cancel the other requests.
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 8.11, **Following the approach of mirroredQuery**, is a practice rep for goroutines in Goroutines and Channels. The surface task is: Following the approach of mirroredQuery in Section 8.4.4, implement a vari- ant of fetch that requests several URLs concurrently. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is a project exercise, there may not be one hidden-test answer. The win is a small reproducible program plus proof that the observed behavior matches the goal.

### Go mental model

- A goroutine is independent work. Make its lifetime and result path visible so it cannot leak or silently fail.

### Tiny check

- Input: Book Exercise 8.11
  Output: A working Go program or function demonstrating the requested behavior.

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. Once the hand prediction is clear, the implementation usually becomes a direct translation: choose the right data shape, loop or call the helper that matches the shape, and return the result without extra cleverness.

### What mastery feels like

You understand this exercise when you can explain why each variable exists, what invariant is maintained while the code runs, and which edge case would break a sloppy solution. Do not just aim for green tests; aim for the moment where the code reads like the problem statement in Go form.

problems.title: Exercise 8.12: Make the broadcaster announce the

problems.title: Exercise 8.13: Make the chat server disconnect
problems.statement: Make the chat server disconnect idle clients, such as those that have sent no messages in the last five minutes. Hint: calling conn.Close() in another goroutine unblocks active Read calls such as the one done by input.Scan().
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 8.13, **Make the chat server disconnect**, is a practice rep for server, goroutine in Goroutines and Channels. The surface task is: Make the chat server disconnect idle clients, such as those that have sent no messages in the last five minutes. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is a project exercise, there may not be one hidden-test answer. The win is a small reproducible program plus proof that the observed behavior matches the goal.

### Go mental model

- Server code should keep handler state explicit. Parse the request, call focused logic, then write one clear response.

- A goroutine is independent work. Make its lifetime and result path visible so it cannot leak or silently fail.

### Tiny check

- Input: Book Exercise 8.13
  Output: A working Go program or function demonstrating the requested behavior.

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. Once the hand prediction is clear, the implementation usually becomes a direct translation: choose the right data shape, loop or call the helper that matches the shape, and return the result without extra cleverness.

### What mastery feels like

You understand this exercise when you can explain why each variable exists, what invariant is maintained while the code runs, and which edge case would break a sloppy solution. Do not just aim for green tests; aim for the moment where the code reads like the problem statement in Go form.

problems.title: Exercise 8.15: Failure of any client program
problems.statement: Failure of any client program to read data in a timely manner ultimately causes all clients to get stuck. Adapt the broadcaster to skip a message rather than wait if a client writer is not ready to accept it. Alternatively, add buffering to each client’s outgoing message channel so that most messages are not dropped; the broadcaster should use a non-blocking send to this channel.
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 8.15, **Failure of any client program**, is a practice rep for writer, goroutine, channel in Goroutines and Channels. The surface task is: Failure of any client program to read data in a timely manner ultimately causes all clients to get stuck. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? - Writers consume bytes and report how much was accepted. Treat errors from Write as part of the result, not as background noise.

- A goroutine is independent work. Make its lifetime and result path visible so it cannot leak or silently fail.

- Channels communicate values and also communicate timing. Ask which goroutine owns sending, which owns receiving, and how the receiver learns that work is done.

- Input: Book Exercise 8.15

problems.title: Exercise 9.1: Bank Balance Serial
problems.statement: Apply deposits and withdrawals and return final balance.
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 9.1, **Bank Balance Serial**, is a practice rep for mutex, state in Shared Variables and Mutexes. The surface task is: Apply deposits and withdrawals and return final balance.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is judgeable, the app can check a focused `Solve` function. That is a gift: you can ignore command-line plumbing for a moment and concentrate on the exact value transformation.

### Go mental model

- A mutex protects an invariant. Keep the locked section small, and make every read or write of the protected state follow the same rule.

- Stateful code is safest when transitions are explicit. Name what changes, when it changes, and what should remain true afterward.

### Tiny check

- Input: [100,-30,5]
  Output: 75

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. Once the hand prediction is clear, the implementation usually becomes a direct translation: choose the right data shape, loop or call the helper that matches the shape, and return the result without extra cleverness.

### What mastery feels like

You understand this exercise when you can explain why each variable exists, what invariant is maintained while the code runs, and which edge case would break a sloppy solution. Do not just aim for green tests; aim for the moment where the code reads like the problem statement in Go form.

problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 9.3, **Memoized Fibonacci**, is a practice rep for memoization, state in Shared Variables and Mutexes. The surface task is: Return the nth Fibonacci number using memo-style thinking.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is judgeable, the app can check a focused `Solve` function. That is a gift: you can ignore command-line plumbing for a moment and concentrate on the exact value transformation.

### Go mental model

- Memoization trades memory for repeated work. The cache key must represent exactly the inputs that affect the result.

- Stateful code is safest when transitions are explicit. Name what changes, when it changes, and what should remain true afterward.

### Tiny check

- Input: 10
  Output: 55

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. Once the hand prediction is clear, the implementation usually becomes a direct translation: choose the right data shape, loop or call the helper that matches the shape, and return the result without extra cleverness.

### What mastery feels like

You understand this exercise when you can explain why each variable exists, what invariant is maintained while the code runs, and which edge case would break a sloppy solution. Do not just aim for green tests; aim for the moment where the code reads like the problem statement in Go form.
problems.approach:
### Solve path for exercise 9.3
1. Rephrase the task in one sentence using your own words.
2. Keep the core function compatible with `func Solve(n int) int`.
3. Work from the smallest sample input first. Predict the output by hand.
4. Implement the direct transformation. Prefer clear names and boring control flow.
5. Run once for feedback, then Submit when the examples and edge cases make sense.
### Self-check before calling it done
- Can you point to the line where the main idea happens?
- Can you explain what happens for an empty, tiny, or boundary-shaped input?
- Did you keep I/O or demonstration code separate from the value transformation where possible?
- If you came back tomorrow, would the variable names remind you of the problem rather than the mechanics?
pitfalls: Changing too much of the original example at once.
Ignoring errors because the happy path worked once.
Treating open-ended book exercises like single-output puzzles instead of reproducible programs.
hints: Find the chapter example this exercise modifies.
Make the smallest visible change first.
Keep a command or sample input that proves your solution works.

problems.solutionCode: func Solve(n int) int { memo:=map[int]int{0:0,1:1}; var f func(int)int; f=func(k int)int{ if v,ok:=memo[k]; ok {return v}; memo[k]=f(k-1)+f(k-2); return memo[k] }; return f(n) }
problems.howItWorks:
### How it works

The target behavior is: Return the nth Fibonacci number using memo-style thinking.

A good solution usually has this flow:

1. Normalize the input into a shape that is easy to reason about.

2. Apply one clear rule repeatedly or directly, depending on the task.

3. Keep intermediate names honest so the code explains what each value means.

4. Return exactly the type promised by the `Solve` signature. Avoid printing from `Solve` unless the signature or task explicitly asks for output text.

5. Let the local tests cover normal and edge cases after your hand-check makes sense.

### Why this approach works

- Memoization trades memory for repeated work. The cache key must represent exactly the inputs that affect the result.

- Stateful code is safest when transitions are explicit. Name what changes, when it changes, and what should remain true afterward.

### Debugging lens

If the answer is wrong, do not randomly rearrange code. Print or inspect the value after each stage of the flow: input shape, transformed shape, final result. Most mistakes in these exercises come from using the right tool at the wrong boundary, or from mixing representation details with the core rule.
problems.syntax:
### Syntax you need
Core shape:
```go
func Solve(n int) int
```
- Keep this exact function name and compatible parameters. The backend judge calls it directly.
### Imports
- No import is required by the starter code unless your implementation chooses a helper package.
### Patterns to reach for
- Memoization trades memory for repeated work. The cache key must represent exactly the inputs that affect the result.
- Stateful code is safest when transitions are explicit. Name what changes, when it changes, and what should remain true afterward.
### Read the signature out loud
The parameters are the only facts your function gets for free. The return type is the promise your function must keep. When you get stuck, read the signature as a sentence and ask what value would satisfy that promise for the smallest sample input.

problems.title: Exercise 9.4: pipeline that connects an arbitrary
problems.statement: Construct a pipeline that connects an arbitrary number of goroutines with chan- nels. What is the maximum number of pipeline stages you can create without running out of memory? How long does a value take to transit the entire pipeline?
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 9.4, **pipeline that connects an arbitrary**, is a practice rep for goroutine in Shared Variables and Mutexes. The surface task is: Construct a pipeline that connects an arbitrary number of goroutines with chan- nels. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is a project exercise, there may not be one hidden-test answer. The win is a small reproducible program plus proof that the observed behavior matches the goal.

### Go mental model

- A goroutine is independent work. Make its lifetime and result path visible so it cannot leak or silently fail.

### Tiny check

- Input: Book Exercise 9.4
  Output: A working Go program or function demonstrating the requested behavior.

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. Once the hand prediction is clear, the implementation usually becomes a direct translation: choose the right data shape, loop or call the helper that matches the shape, and return the result without extra cleverness.

### What mastery feels like

You understand this exercise when you can explain why each variable exists, what invariant is maintained while the code runs, and which edge case would break a sloppy solution. Do not just aim for green tests; aim for the moment where the code reads like the problem statement in Go form.

problems.title: Exercise 9.5: program with two goroutines that
problems.statement: Build a program with two goroutines that send messages back and forth over two unbuffered channels in ping-pong fashion. How many communications per second can the program sustain?
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 9.5, **program with two goroutines that**, is a practice rep for goroutine, channel in Shared Variables and Mutexes. The surface task is: Build a program with two goroutines that send messages back and forth over two unbuffered channels in ping-pong fashion. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is a project exercise, there may not be one hidden-test answer. The win is a small reproducible program plus proof that the observed behavior matches the goal.

### Go mental model

- A goroutine is independent work. Make its lifetime and result path visible so it cannot leak or silently fail.

- Channels communicate values and also communicate timing. Ask which goroutine owns sending, which owns receiving, and how the receiver learns that work is done.

### Tiny check

- Input: Book Exercise 9.5
  Output: A working Go program or function demonstrating the requested behavior.

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. ### What mastery feels like

You understand this exercise when you can explain why each variable exists, what invariant is maintained while the code runs, and which edge case would break a sloppy solution. Do not just aim for green tests; aim for the moment where the code reads like the problem statement in Go form.

problems.statement: Measure how the performance of a compute-bound parallel program (see Exer- cise 8.5) varies with GOMAXPROCS. What is the optimal value on your computer? How many CPUs does your computer have?

problems.title: Exercise 10.1: Package Path Base

problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is judgeable, the app can check a focused `Solve` function. That is a gift: you can ignore command-line plumbing for a moment and concentrate on the exact value transformation.

### Go mental model

- Packages draw boundaries. A good package exposes the vocabulary callers need while hiding the details they should not depend on.

- Strings are immutable byte sequences. Decide whether the task is about bytes, runes, words, prefixes, or joined pieces before choosing helpers.

### Tiny check

- Input: github.com/u/repo/pkg
  Output: pkg

problems.solutionCode:
import "strings"
func Solve(path string) string { path=strings.Trim(path,"/"); if path=="" {return ""}; parts:=strings.Split(path,"/"); return parts[len(parts)-1] }

examples.output: pkg

problems.title: Exercise 10.2: Package Import Groups
problems.statement: Group import paths as standard library or external based on whether they contain a dot.
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 10.2, **Package Import Groups**, is a practice rep for packages, sort in Packages and the Go Tool. The surface task is: Group import paths as standard library or external based on whether they contain a dot.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? problems.solutionCode:
import ("sort"; "strings")
func Solve(paths []string) map[string][]string { out:=map[string][]string{"std":{},"external":{}}; for _,p:=range paths { if strings.Contains(strings.Split(p,"/")[0],".") {out["external"]=append(out["external"],p)} else {out["std"]=append(out["std"],p)} }; sort.Strings(out["std"]); sort.Strings(out["external"]); return out }

examples.output: std/external groups

problems.statement: Using fetch http://gopl.io/ch1/helloworld?go-get=1, find out which service hosts the code samples for this book. (HTTP requests from go get include the go-get parameter so that servers can distinguish them from ordinary browser requests.)
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 10.3, **Using fetch http gopl io**, is a practice rep for http, server in Packages and the Go Tool. The surface task is: Using fetch http://gopl.io/ch1/helloworld?go-get=1, find out which service hosts the code samples for this book. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Because this one is a project exercise, there may not be one hidden-test answer. The win is a small reproducible program plus proof that the observed behavior matches the goal.

### Go mental model

- HTTP code has two halves: constructing the request and interpreting the response. Keep errors, status, headers, and body handling distinct.

- Server code should keep handler state explicit. Parse the request, call focused logic, then write one clear response.

problems.explanation:
### What you are learning

Exercise 10.4, **tool that reports the set**, is a practice rep for json, test in Packages and the Go Tool. The surface task is: Construct a tool that reports the set of all packages in the workspace that tran- sitively depend on the packages specified by the arguments. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is a project exercise, there may not be one hidden-test answer. The win is a small reproducible program plus proof that the observed behavior matches the goal.

### Go mental model

- JSON work is about mapping between Go fields and encoded names. Make the struct shape and tags tell the story.

- Tests are executable examples. They are strongest when the input, expected output, and edge case each communicate one idea.

### Tiny check

- Input: Book Exercise 10.4
  Output: A working Go program or function demonstrating the requested behavior.

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. Once the hand prediction is clear, the implementation usually becomes a direct translation: choose the right data shape, loop or call the helper that matches the shape, and return the result without extra cleverness.

### What mastery feels like

You understand this exercise when you can explain why each variable exists, what invariant is maintained while the code runs, and which edge case would break a sloppy solution. Do not just aim for green tests; aim for the moment where the code reads like the problem statement in Go form.
problems.solutionCode:
Manual project guidance for exercise 10.4:

- Build the full program described in the Study and Problem tabs.
- Run it with a small reproducible input or command.
- Record the command, any sample files, and the observed output.
- Mark the project complete only after the proof matches the exercise goal.

examples.output: A working Go program or function demonstrating the requested behavior.

problems.howItWorks:
### How it works

The target behavior is: Construct a tool that reports the set of all packages in the workspace that tran- sitively depend on the packages specified by the arguments. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

A good solution usually has this flow:

1. Normalize the input into a shape that is easy to reason about.

2. Apply one clear rule repeatedly or directly, depending on the task.

3. Keep intermediate names honest so the code explains what each value means.

4. Build the smallest runnable demonstration that shows the behavior. For project exercises, the proof matters as much as the code.

5. Record the command, input, and observed output so you can tell whether later edits changed the behavior.

### Why this approach works

- JSON work is about mapping between Go fields and encoded names. Make the struct shape and tags tell the story.

- Tests are executable examples. They are strongest when the input, expected output, and edge case each communicate one idea.

### Debugging lens

If the answer is wrong, do not randomly rearrange code. Print or inspect the value after each stage of the flow: input shape, transformed shape, final result. Most mistakes in these exercises come from using the right tool at the wrong boundary, or from mixing representation details with the core rule.
problems.syntax:
### Syntax you need
Core shape:
```go
func Solve() string
```
- For a project entry, you may keep this shape as a helper, but you can also add a small `main` when you need a runnable proof.
### Imports
- No import is required by the starter code unless your implementation chooses a helper package.
### Patterns to reach for
- JSON work is about mapping between Go fields and encoded names. Make the struct shape and tags tell the story.
- Tests are executable examples. They are strongest when the input, expected output, and edge case each communicate one idea.
### Read the signature out loud
The parameters are the only facts your function gets for free. The return type is the promise your function must keep. When you get stuck, read the signature as a sentence and ask what value would satisfy that promise for the smallest sample input.

Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.
problems.solve:
### Solve path for exercise 10.4
1. Rephrase the task in one sentence using your own words.
2. Start with the smallest runnable version, even if it only handles one tiny case.
3. Run it and write down the command or input you used.
4. Expand the implementation until it demonstrates the full behavior.
5. Keep the proof: command, sample input or file, observed output, and one note explaining why it satisfies the exercise.
### Self-check before calling it done
- Can you point to the line where the main idea happens?
- Can you explain what happens for an empty, tiny, or boundary-shaped input?
- Did you keep I/O or demonstration code separate from the value transformation where possible?
- If you came back tomorrow, would the variable names remind you of the problem rather than the mechanics?

problems.solutionCode:
import "unicode"
func Solve(s string) map[string]int { m:=map[string]int{"letters":0,"digits":0,"spaces":0}; for _,r:=range s { if unicode.IsLetter(r){m["letters"]++}; if unicode.IsDigit(r){m["digits"]++}; if unicode.IsSpace(r){m["spaces"]++} }; return m }

examples.output: letters=2 digits=1 spaces=1

problems.solutionCode:
func rev(xs []int){ for i,j:=0,len(xs)-1;i<j;i,j=i+1,j-1 {xs[i],xs[j]=xs[j],xs[i]} }
func Solve(xs []int) []int { out:=append([]int{},xs...); rev(out); rev(out); return out }

problems.solutionCode:
import "reflect"
func Solve(v any) string { if v==nil {return "invalid"}; return reflect.TypeOf(v).Kind().String() }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve(3),"int"); assertEqual(Solve("x"),"string"); assertEqual(Solve([]int{}),"slice"); assertEqual(Solve(nil),"invalid") }

examples.output: slice

problems.solutionCode:
import "reflect"
func Solve(v any) int { if v==nil {return -1}; rv:=reflect.ValueOf(v); switch rv.Kind(){ case reflect.Slice,reflect.Array,reflect.Map,reflect.String: return rv.Len(); default: return -1 } }

problems.title: Exercise 12.3: the missing cases of the
problems.statement: Implement the missing cases of the encode function. Encode booleans as t and nil, floating-point numbers using Go’s notation, and complex numbers like 1+2i as #C(1.0 2.0). Interfaces can be encoded as a pair of a type name and a value, for instance ("[]int" (1 2 3)), but beware that this notation is ambiguous: the reflect.Type.String method may return the same string for different types.

The target behavior is: Adapt encode to pretty-print the S-expression in the style shown above. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

A good solution usually has this flow:

1. Normalize the input into a shape that is easy to reason about.

2. Apply one clear rule repeatedly or directly, depending on the task.

3. Keep intermediate names honest so the code explains what each value means.

4. Build the smallest runnable demonstration that shows the behavior. For project exercises, the proof matters as much as the code.

5. Record the command, input, and observed output so you can tell whether later edits changed the behavior.

### Why this approach works

- Reflection is for code that must inspect values whose concrete type is not known at compile time. Always check Kind before assuming operations are valid.

### Debugging lens

If the answer is wrong, do not randomly rearrange code. Print or inspect the value after each stage of the flow: input shape, transformed shape, final result. Most mistakes in these exercises come from using the right tool at the wrong boundary, or from mixing representation details with the core rule.

problems.title: Exercise 12.5: Adapt encode to emit JSON
problems.statement: Adapt encode to emit JSON instead of S-expressions. Test your encoder using the standard decoder, json.Unmarshal.
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 12.5, **Adapt encode to emit JSON**, is a practice rep for json, test in Reflection. The surface task is: Adapt encode to emit JSON instead of S-expressions. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is a project exercise, there may not be one hidden-test answer. The win is a small reproducible program plus proof that the observed behavior matches the goal.

### Go mental model

- JSON work is about mapping between Go fields and encoded names. Make the struct shape and tags tell the story.

- Tests are executable examples. They are strongest when the input, expected output, and edge case each communicate one idea.

### Tiny check

- Input: Book Exercise 12.5
  Output: A working Go program or function demonstrating the requested behavior.

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. Once the hand prediction is clear, the implementation usually becomes a direct translation: choose the right data shape, loop or call the helper that matches the shape, and return the result without extra cleverness.

### What mastery feels like

You understand this exercise when you can explain why each variable exists, what invariant is maintained while the code runs, and which edge case would break a sloppy solution. Do not just aim for green tests; aim for the moment where the code reads like the problem statement in Go form.

problems.title: Exercise 12.6: Struct Field Names
problems.statement: Return exported field names of a struct value in declaration order.
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 12.6, **Struct Field Names**, is a practice rep for reflection, structs in Reflection. The surface task is: Return exported field names of a struct value in declaration order.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is judgeable, the app can check a focused `Solve` function. That is a gift: you can ignore command-line plumbing for a moment and concentrate on the exact value transformation.

### Go mental model

- Reflection is for code that must inspect values whose concrete type is not known at compile time. Always check Kind before assuming operations are valid.

- Structs give names to related fields. Use those names to make illegal or confusing data shapes harder to create.

### Tiny check

- Input: User{Name, age, Email}
  Output: [Name, Email]

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. Once the hand prediction is clear, the implementation usually becomes a direct translation: choose the right data shape, loop or call the helper that matches the shape, and return the result without extra cleverness.

### What mastery feels like

You understand this exercise when you can explain why each variable exists, what invariant is maintained while the code runs, and which edge case would break a sloppy solution. Do not just aim for green tests; aim for the moment where the code reads like the problem statement in Go form.

5. Run once for feedback, then Submit when the examples and edge cases make sense.

problems.solutionCode:
import "reflect"
func Solve(v any) []string { t:=reflect.TypeOf(v); if t==nil {return nil}; if t.Kind()==reflect.Pointer {t=t.Elem()}; if t.Kind()!=reflect.Struct {return nil}; out:=[]string{}; for i:=0;i<t.NumField();i++ { f:=t.Field(i); if f.PkgPath=="" {out=append(out,f.Name)} }; return out }
problems.testCode:
type User struct{Name string; age int; Email string}
func main(){ assertEqual(Solve(User{}),[]string{"Name","Email"}); assertEqual(Solve(3),[]string(nil)) }

examples.output: [Name, Email]

problems.title: Exercise 12.8: sexpr Unmarshal function like json
problems.statement: The sexpr.Unmarshal function, like json.Marshal, requires the complete input in a byte slice before it can begin decoding. Define a sexpr.Decoder type that, like json.Decoder, allows a sequence of values to be decoded from an io.Reader. Change sexpr.Unmarshal to use this new type.
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 12.8, **sexpr Unmarshal function like json**, is a practice rep for json, slice, reader in Reflection. The surface task is: The sexpr.Unmarshal function, like json.Marshal, requires the complete input in a byte slice before it can begin decoding. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? ### Intuition

The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is a project exercise, there may not be one hidden-test answer. The win is a small reproducible program plus proof that the observed behavior matches the goal.

### Go mental model

- JSON work is about mapping between Go fields and encoded names. Make the struct shape and tags tell the story.

- Slices are views over arrays. Track length, capacity, and whether two slices might share the same backing array.

- Readers stream bytes in chunks. Code that uses an io.Reader should work even when data arrives in small pieces.

problems.explanation:
### What you are learning

Exercise 12.8, **sexpr Unmarshal function like json**, is a practice rep for json, slice, reader in Reflection. The surface task is: The sexpr.Unmarshal function, like json.Marshal, requires the complete input in a byte slice before it can begin decoding. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is a project exercise, there may not be one hidden-test answer. The win is a small reproducible program plus proof that the observed behavior matches the goal.

### Go mental model

- JSON work is about mapping between Go fields and encoded names. Make the struct shape and tags tell the story.

- Slices are views over arrays. Track length, capacity, and whether two slices might share the same backing array.

- Readers stream bytes in chunks. Code that uses an io.Reader should work even when data arrives in small pieces.

### Tiny check

- Input: Book Exercise 12.8
  Output: A working Go program or function demonstrating the requested behavior.

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. Once the hand prediction is clear, the implementation usually becomes a direct translation: choose the right data shape, loop or call the helper that matches the shape, and return the result without extra cleverness.

### What mastery feels like

You understand this exercise when you can explain why each variable exists, what invariant is maintained while the code runs, and which edge case would break a sloppy solution. Do not just aim for green tests; aim for the moment where the code reads like the problem statement in Go form.

problems.howItWorks:
### How it works

The target behavior is: The sexpr.Unmarshal function, like json.Marshal, requires the complete input in a byte slice before it can begin decoding. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

A good solution usually has this flow:

1. Normalize the input into a shape that is easy to reason about.

2. Apply one clear rule repeatedly or directly, depending on the task.

3. Keep intermediate names honest so the code explains what each value means.

4. Build the smallest runnable demonstration that shows the behavior. For project exercises, the proof matters as much as the code.

5. Record the command, input, and observed output so you can tell whether later edits changed the behavior.

### Why this approach works

- JSON work is about mapping between Go fields and encoded names. Make the struct shape and tags tell the story.

- Slices are views over arrays. Track length, capacity, and whether two slices might share the same backing array.

- Readers stream bytes in chunks. Code that uses an io.Reader should work even when data arrives in small pieces.

### Debugging lens

If the answer is wrong, do not randomly rearrange code. Print or inspect the value after each stage of the flow: input shape, transformed shape, final result. Most mistakes in these exercises come from using the right tool at the wrong boundary, or from mixing representation details with the core rule.

### Imports
- No import is required by the starter code unless your implementation chooses a helper package.
### Patterns to reach for
- JSON work is about mapping between Go fields and encoded names. Make the struct shape and tags tell the story.
- Slices are views over arrays. Track length, capacity, and whether two slices might share the same backing array.
- Readers stream bytes in chunks. Code that uses an io.Reader should work even when data arrives in small pieces.
### Read the signature out loud
The parameters are the only facts your function gets for free. The return type is the promise your function must keep. When you get stuck, read the signature as a sentence and ask what value would satisfy that promise for the smallest sample input.

problems.title: Exercise 12.9: token based API for decoding

problems.title: Exercise 12.10: sexpr Unmarshal to handle the
problems.statement: Extend sexpr.Unmarshal to handle the booleans, floating-point numbers, and interfaces encoded by your solution to Exercise 12.3. (Hint: to decode interfaces, you will need a mapping from the name of each supported type to its reflect.Type.)
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 12.10, **sexpr Unmarshal to handle the**, is a practice rep for map, interface, reflect in Reflection. The surface task is: Extend sexpr.Unmarshal to handle the booleans, floating-point numbers, and interfaces encoded by your solution to Exercise 12.3. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is a project exercise, there may not be one hidden-test answer. The win is a small reproducible program plus proof that the observed behavior matches the goal.

### Go mental model

- Maps are for lookup, grouping, counting, and deduplication. Decide what the key means and what invariant each value should maintain.

- Interfaces describe required behavior. Name the method set you need, then let concrete types satisfy it without ceremony.

- Reflection is for code that must inspect values whose concrete type is not known at compile time. Always check Kind before assuming operations are valid.

### Tiny check

- Input: Book Exercise 12.10
  Output: A working Go program or function demonstrating the requested behavior.

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. Once the hand prediction is clear, the implementation usually becomes a direct translation: choose the right data shape, loop or call the helper that matches the shape, and return the result without extra cleverness.

problems.title: Exercise 13.1: Size Class
problems.statement: Classify byte size as tiny (<=1), small (<=8), medium (<=64), or large.
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 13.1, **Size Class**, is a practice rep for unsafe, memory in Low-Level Programming. The surface task is: Classify byte size as tiny (<=1), small (<=8), medium (<=64), or large.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is judgeable, the app can check a focused `Solve` function. That is a gift: you can ignore command-line plumbing for a moment and concentrate on the exact value transformation.

### Go mental model

- Unsafe code bypasses Go safety. Use it only when the representation question is the point, and keep the unsafe boundary tiny.

- Memory-layout problems ask what Go stores, not what the code visually resembles. Think in size, alignment, padding, and representation.

### Tiny check

- Input: 8
  Output: small

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious. ### What mastery feels like

problems.solutionCode: func Solve(size,align uintptr) uintptr { if align==0 {return size}; r:=size%align; if r==0 {return size}; return size+(align-r) }
problems.howItWorks:
### How it works

The target behavior is: Round a size up to the next multiple of alignment.

A good solution usually has this flow:

1. Normalize the input into a shape that is easy to reason about.

2. Apply one clear rule repeatedly or directly, depending on the task.

3. Keep intermediate names honest so the code explains what each value means.

4. Return exactly the type promised by the `Solve` signature. Avoid printing from `Solve` unless the signature or task explicitly asks for output text.

5. Let the local tests cover normal and edge cases after your hand-check makes sense.

- Unsafe code bypasses Go safety. Use it only when the representation question is the point, and keep the unsafe boundary tiny.

- Alignment is about placing sizes on predictable boundaries. Think in remainders: if the value is already on a boundary, keep it; otherwise add the gap to the next boundary.

problems.title: Exercise 13.3: sync Mutex to make bzip2
problems.statement: Practice using sync.Mutex to make bzip2.writer safe for concurrent use by multiple goroutines.
problems.lesson:
### What you are learning

Exercise 13.3, **sync Mutex to make bzip2**, is a practice rep for writer, goroutine, mutex in Low-Level Programming. The surface task is: Practice using sync.Mutex to make bzip2.writer safe for concurrent use by multiple goroutines. Produce a short reproducible proof: the command or input you used and the observed output or behavior.

The deeper lesson is learning how to turn a paragraph-sized programming request into a small Go-shaped decision. Before typing code, slow down and ask: What are the inputs? What should come out? Which part is pure transformation, and which part is just I/O, formatting, or demonstration? That question is the difference between guessing at code and designing a solution you can explain.

### Intuition

Think of the problem as a pipeline. Something enters the left side, your code applies one clear rule, and a result leaves the right side. If the exercise mentions files, HTTP, command-line arguments, reflection, or concurrency, those are still just boundaries around the core idea. The trick is to keep the boundary visible instead of letting it blur the actual operation.

Because this one is a project exercise, there may not be one hidden-test answer. The win is a small reproducible program plus proof that the observed behavior matches the goal.

### Go mental model

- Writers consume bytes and report how much was accepted. Treat errors from Write as part of the result, not as background noise.

- A goroutine is independent work. Make its lifetime and result path visible so it cannot leak or silently fail.

- A mutex protects an invariant. Keep the locked section small, and make every read or write of the protected state follow the same rule.

### Tiny check

- Input: Book Exercise 13.3
  Output: A working Go program or function demonstrating the requested behavior.

Use the tiny check before chasing a complete solution. If you cannot predict the output for a small input by hand, the code will feel mysterious.
